fields.name: Major Depressive Disorder (MDD)
fields.definition: A complex mood disorder caused by various factors, including genetic predisposition, personality, stress, and brain chemistry. CAMH emphasizes that clinical depression is much more than simple unhappiness and is not something people can 'get over' by their own effort. To meet diagnostic criteria, a person must experience five or more specified symptoms for at least two weeks, including depressed mood and/or loss of interest, along with changes in sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, self-worth, or thoughts of death. In Canada, about 5% of people aged 15 and older have experienced a major depressive episode in the past year, and about 11% of adults will meet criteria for MDD in their lifetime.
fields.example: A person feels persistently hopeless, loses interest in hobbies they previously enjoyed, sleeps 12 hours a day, and cannot concentrate at work, with symptoms lasting more than 3 weeks.

fields.name: Persistent Depressive Disorder (PDD)
fields.definition: A chronic form of depression featuring depressive symptoms that last for at least two continuous years, with periods of remission lasting no longer than two consecutive months. CAMH's professional guidelines note that PDD symptoms can be subsyndromal (fewer or less severe than a full major depressive episode) or syndromal (meeting full criteria for depression for at least two years). Because symptoms are chronic and may be normalized by the person experiencing them, PDD is often underrecognized. People with PDD may experience major depressive episodes on top of their baseline chronic low mood, sometimes called 'double depression.'
fields.example: A person who has felt 'down most of the time' for 3 years, struggles with low energy and poor self-esteem, but has not had a severe enough episode to clearly identify as major depression.

fields.name: Seasonal Affective Disorder (SAD)
fields.definition: A type of depression usually affected by the weather and time of year. CAMH identifies it as a distinct form of depression that develops under specific seasonal circumstances. NIMH notes that SAD most commonly occurs in fall and winter when daylight hours are shorter, though a less common form occurs in spring and summer. Symptoms include low energy, oversleeping, overeating, weight gain, and social withdrawal. Light therapy (exposure to bright artificial light) is a common treatment. SAD is more prevalent in northern latitudes where seasonal changes in daylight are more extreme.
fields.example: A person living in northern Canada experiences reliably worsening mood, increased sleep, and social withdrawal every November that lifts by April.

fields.name: Postpartum and Perinatal Depression
fields.definition: Depression occurring during pregnancy or after childbirth. CAMH notes that many hormonal factors may contribute to higher rates of depression in women, particularly during pregnancy, the postpartum period, miscarriage, pre-menopause, and menopause. NIMH describes perinatal depression as more than the 'baby blues,' which are mild symptoms that typically resolve within two weeks. Perinatal depression involves severe symptoms that can interfere with the ability to care for oneself and the baby. It can occur during pregnancy (prenatal) or after delivery (postpartum) and requires professional treatment.
fields.example: A new parent experiences overwhelming sadness, difficulty bonding with their baby, severe fatigue, and thoughts of self-harm in the weeks following delivery.

fields.name: Depression with Psychosis
fields.definition: A severe form of depression in which a person loses touch with reality and experiences hallucinations (hearing voices or seeing things that are not there) or delusions (beliefs that have no basis in reality). CAMH describes this as occurring when depression becomes so severe that psychotic features emerge. NIMH notes that the psychotic symptoms typically have a depressive theme, such as delusions of guilt, worthlessness, or illness. Depression with psychosis is a psychiatric emergency that requires immediate treatment, often including both antidepressant and antipsychotic medication.
fields.example: A person with severe depression begins hearing a voice telling them they are worthless and that their family would be better off without them.

fields.name: Antidepressant Medication
fields.definition: Medications that work by affecting brain chemistry to relieve depressive symptoms. CAMH and NIMH both note that treatments for depression include psychoeducation, psychotherapy, pharmacotherapy, and brain stimulation therapies (such as ECT, rTMS, and MST). These may be used individually or in combination. CAMH emphasizes that full remission of symptoms is an important goal for both acute and maintenance treatment, and that clinicians should monitor treatment response and coach patients in self-management. Antidepressants typically take several weeks to show full effect. CAMH warns that alcohol, street drugs, and some prescription medications can provide temporary relief from symptoms but are not effective treatments.
fields.example: A person starts an SSRI for depression and, over 4 to 6 weeks, notices gradual improvement in sleep, appetite, and ability to concentrate.

fields.name: Psychotherapy for Depression
fields.definition: Talk therapy approaches that help people identify and change negative thinking patterns and behaviors contributing to depression. CAMH identifies cognitive-behavioural therapy (CBT) as a structured approach in which members work through a step-by-step process to address attitudes and behaviors that maintain depression. Other effective approaches include interpersonal therapy (focused on improving relationships and communication) and long-term psychodynamic therapy (exploring how past experiences affect current functioning). CAMH notes that peer support groups can be a valuable part of the social network needed for recovery, and self-help organizations run by clients and families can be an important part of treatment.
fields.example: A person in CBT for depression learns to identify automatic negative thoughts like 'I'm worthless' and challenge them with evidence, gradually rebuilding a more balanced perspective.

fields.question: How does depression differ between men and women?
fields.answer: CAMH notes that depression is more common in women, though the gender difference diminishes with age in Canada. Hormonal factors during menstrual cycle changes, pregnancy, postpartum, and menopause may contribute to higher rates in women. Men with depression typically have higher rates of feeling irritable, angry, and discouraged, which can make depression harder to recognize in men. The rate of completed suicide in men is four times that in women, although more women attempt suicide. These differences highlight the importance of recognizing that depression can present differently across genders.

fields.question: How is depression different from grief?
fields.answer: CAMH's professional guidelines identify several key differences. Symptom severity: depression involves more severe symptoms, such as psychosis and suicidality. Reported affect: the predominant feeling in grief is emptiness and loss, whereas in depression it is persistent depressed mood. Course: dysphoria in grief decreases in intensity over days or weeks and comes in waves, while depressed mood in depression is more persistent and pervasive. DSM-5 removed the bereavement exclusion for depression, acknowledging that grief can coexist with or trigger depression. However, persistent complex bereavement disorder may be considered when severe grief symptoms persist beyond 12 months.

fields.question: How common is depression?
fields.answer: Depression is one of the most common mental health conditions. CAMH reports that about 5% of Canadians aged 15 and older have experienced a major depressive episode in the past year, and about 11% will meet criteria for MDD in their lifetime. The prevalence is highest among working-age adults (15 to 64), making it one of the leading causes of work-related disability and lost productivity. CAMH's broader statistics note that by age 40, 1 in 2 Canadians have or have had a mental illness. NIMH data shows that approximately 8.3% of U.S. adults had at least one major depressive episode in 2021.

fields.question: Can depression come back after treatment?
fields.answer: Yes. CAMH emphasizes that the best protection against relapse is understanding that depression is an illness that must be managed over a lifetime, even during periods of health. Watching for early warning signs of relapse may prevent a full depressive episode. CAMH compares depression to chronic disorders like diabetes: it can be effectively managed and controlled by combining a healthy lifestyle with treatments. Strategies for preventing relapse include maintaining treatment even when feeling well, developing a balanced life rather than escaping into a single focus like work, building a strong social support network, and having at least one person to confide in.

fields.question: What conditions commonly co-occur with depression?
fields.answer: Depression frequently co-occurs with anxiety disorders, especially generalized anxiety disorder, social anxiety disorder, and panic disorder. CAMH notes that the combination of depression and severe anxiety is particularly concerning because it increases the risk of suicidality. Depression also commonly accompanies substance use disorders, chronic pain, and other physical health conditions. CAMH's broader statistics note that people with a mood disorder are at much higher risk of developing long-term medical conditions, and conversely, people with chronic physical conditions are more likely to experience mood disorders. Many medications as well as alcohol can cause depressive symptoms.

fields.question: What are brain stimulation therapies for depression?
fields.answer: CAMH identifies three brain stimulation treatments used for depression: electroconvulsive therapy (ECT), repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), and magnetic seizure therapy (MST). These are typically used when medication and psychotherapy have not been sufficient. ECT involves brief electrical stimulation of the brain while the patient is under anesthesia, and is one of the most effective treatments for severe or treatment-resistant depression. rTMS uses magnetic pulses targeted at specific brain areas involved in mood regulation. MST is a newer approach being researched at CAMH. These treatments can be used individually or combined with medication and therapy.

fields.question: Can people with depression just 'snap out of it' or 'think positive'?
fields.answer: No. CAMH states explicitly that clinical depression is not something people can 'get over' by their own effort. While it can suddenly go into remission, depression is a medical condition with neurobiological underpinnings involving genetic predisposition, personality, stress, and brain chemistry. It is not the same as normal sadness. CAMH notes that many people try to cope on their own while feeling their mood 'slipping,' sometimes for months or years, until it reaches an intolerable point. Depression requires professional treatment, just as any other serious medical condition does.
